domain: Invoicing SaaS app for freelancers and small agencies: clients, invoices, payments

messages.text: three of my clients are on monthly retainers and I re-create the same invoice by hand every month like an animal. can the app do this for me?

It absolutely should — recurring invoices are the single biggest time-saver an invoicing tool can offer, and retainers are the textbook case. The shape of the feature is a schedule attached to a client: cadence, amount or line items, a start date, optionally an end date. Cadence is where most tools over-build; weekly, monthly, and quarterly cover nearly everyone, and monthly alone covers most. The genuinely tricky questions are not the schedule mechanics but the lifecycle around each occurrence: when exactly an occurrence gets generated, whether it goes out on its own or waits for a human, what happens when a schedule changes mid-stream, and how pausing works when a client goes quiet. Those are product decisions more than engineering ones, and I would rather we make them deliberately than inherit whatever a first draft happens to do. What cadences do you actually need today?
monthly is the only one I need right now honestly

Then we start monthly-only and keep the door open. Each schedule anchors to a day of the month — the 1st and the 15th are the popular anchors — and months without that day roll to the last day, so an anchor on the 31st still fires in February. End dates stay optional since retainers are usually open-ended. Keeping it to one cadence also keeps the settings screen honest: one dropdown fewer, one bug surface fewer.
what happens if a retainer changes price mid stream

The clean model is that a schedule edit affects future occurrences only — anything already generated keeps the terms it was generated with, because a document a client may have already seen must never mutate underneath them. So a price change edits the schedule, and the next occurrence picks it up; if you need the change to hit an occurrence that already exists you edit that occurrence directly, same as any draft invoice. That split — schedules own the future, occurrences own themselves — resolves basically every mid-stream question the same way, including pausing, which is just the schedule declining to produce occurrences for a while. The two questions I still do not want to answer casually are the generation timing and whether anything ever sends without a human looking at it. Those deserve their own conversation once the payments work calms down, because the wrong default there erodes trust fast.
yeah park the fancy stuff til after the payments work. but I need SOMETHING for the retainers now

Understood — I'll wire the bare-bones stopgap now: it creates and sends each invoice the moment its scheduled date arrives, no review step, explicitly temporary until we design the proper flow. Your three retainers stop being manual this month, and I've written down the two open questions so they don't get lost when we come back to this.
should we even charge late fees? half my invoices get paid late and I eat it every time

It's worth deciding deliberately, because both answers are defensible. The case for charging: a consequence, even a small one, moves your invoice up the client's payment queue, and businesses that charge something report meaningfully shorter collection times. The case against: a fee lands on the relationship, not just the ledger, and for anchor clients the goodwill cost of a surprise charge can exceed a year of fees. There's also a third path people forget — a small early-payment discount, the carrot instead of the stick, which some businesses find outperforms any penalty because it reframes the whole exchange as a favor rather than a threat. The honest prerequisite for any of these is knowing your actual overdue pattern: how many invoices go late, how late, and whether it's the same two clients every time. Do you have a feel for that, or is it a blur?
kind of a blur. a flat fee like $15 would at least be simple to explain

Simple, yes, but flat fees are regressive in a way clients notice: the same charge on a tiny invoice reads as spiteful while on a large one it's invisible, so it annoys exactly the clients least worth annoying. A percentage-based charge scales with the harm, which is easier to defend when someone pushes back. Neither is obviously right without knowing your invoice size distribution.
what about just charging interest like a credit card does

That's the accruing model — a percentage added for each period the balance stays outstanding — and it has real advantages: pressure that grows the longer someone waits, no single cliff to argue about, and a familiar mental model. Two cautions. Legally, several jurisdictions cap the rate you can charge on trade debt, so whatever rate anyone picks needs to sit comfortably under those caps, and it should be disclosed on the invoice itself rather than sprung later. Practically, the accrual math needs to be visible — a client who watches a balance drift upward without an itemized explanation assumes a bug or a scam, and either assumption costs you an email thread. But the honest answer is that we don't yet have the overdue data to know whether any of this changes behavior for your clients specifically, and picking a mechanism blind is how policies end up ignored.
yeah lets wait and see how many actually go overdue before picking anything

Agreed — parking it. I'll add a small report tracking how many invoices go overdue each month and for how long, so when we revisit we're choosing with data instead of vibes. No fee of any kind is configured in the meantime, and reminder emails stay purely informational until we decide otherwise.
everyone uses stripe right? maybe we should just use stripe when we add card payments

Stripe is the default answer for a reason, and it's worth a serious look. The strengths are real: documentation that answers questions before you ask them, a brand clients recognize on a checkout page, an enormous ecosystem of libraries, and operational reliability nobody seriously questions. If we were building a generic checkout, I'd sign today. My hesitation is specific to what we are: an invoicing product. Their primitives are charges and payment intents, and mapping those cleanly back onto invoices — partial payments, balances that include fees, remittance references — is plumbing we'd own forever. There's a category of smaller invoicing-first gateways whose entire pitch is that the invoice is the native object, and I think we owe ourselves a look at one or two of those before committing either way. Want me to set up a Stripe sandbox in the meantime so we're comparing something concrete?
yeah do the sandbox. their fee page looked reasonable when I skimmed it

Fees genuinely won't be the deciding factor — at your volume every serious provider lands within a rounding error of the others, and the published rates converge more every year. The decision should hinge on integration fit: how much translation code sits between their events and our invoices, and who owns reconciliation edge cases. Sandbox is up; I'll wire a throwaway pay button so we can feel the flow end to end.
played with the sandbox checkout. it IS slick I'll admit

It is — the hosted checkout is probably the most polished surface in the industry, and if a client's only touchpoint were that page I'd stop evaluating. The friction shows up behind it: to tie a payment back to an invoice we'd stuff our reference into metadata, then translate their webhook vocabulary into ours, and partial payments would be entirely our bookkeeping on top of their charges. None of that is hard, but all of it is ours to maintain forever, and it's exactly the layer the invoice-first gateways claim to delete. So my recommendation stands: line up demos with a couple of the invoicing-focused options next week, then decide with both in front of us rather than extrapolating from one sandbox afternoon. If the smaller players disappoint, we come back here with clear eyes and no regrets.
ok fair, no decision yet then. book the demos

Booked — two invoice-first gateway demos next week, and the Stripe sandbox stays alive so we can compare side by side while it's fresh. Holding off on any real build until we've picked; nothing is committed yet.
ok the overdue report you added is depressing. invoices just sit there and clients ignore them and I look like a pushover. we said we'd revisit charging something — lets actually do it

Good — and the report gives us the justification we wanted. There are three models to choose between, and they feel very different on the receiving end. A flat fee is simple to explain but punishes small invoices: the same charge on an $80 invoice reads as hostile while on an $8,000 one it's a rounding error. A one-time percentage scales with the amount, but once it's been applied the client has no further reason to hurry. The third option is a monthly accruing percentage — effectively interest: each month the balance stays unpaid, a percentage of what's outstanding gets added. That keeps gentle pressure on without a single scary cliff, and it's what most bookkeeping tools default to. One caveat: some jurisdictions cap late-payment interest, so the rate should stay modest, and we should print it on the invoice so nobody can claim surprise. Which direction feels right for your clients?
the interest style one. flat fees always felt petty to me. what rate do people actually use?

Most tools land somewhere between one and three percent per month, and anything above that starts triggering awkward conversations with clients who feel gouged. Below one percent nobody notices it exists, so it fails as an incentive. I'd stay under three, pick a slightly non-round number so it looks considered rather than arbitrary, and state it plainly in the invoice footer. Want me to just pick?
yeah you pick, somewhere under 3 like you said. just make sure it shows on the invoice itself

Locked in: overdue invoices accrue a late fee of 2.75% per month, calculated on the outstanding balance, and the rate is printed in the invoice footer so it never comes as a surprise. I went with 2.75% because it sits in the credible middle of the range — noticeable enough to change behavior, low enough that nobody disputes it on principle, and comfortably under every interest cap I checked. Partial payments reduce the balance the fee is computed on, which is both the fair reading and the one that encourages clients to pay something rather than nothing. Each month the fee accrues it lands as its own ledger line item, so statements stay auditable instead of the total silently drifting upward. The accrual entry looks like this:

```sql
INSERT INTO ledger_entries (invoice_id, kind, amount_cents)
VALUES (?, 'late_fee_accrual', ?);
```

A nightly job walks past-due invoices and applies it; nothing touches invoices that aren't overdue.
great. unrelated-ish: the overdue notice itself looks kind of amateur. the invoice number on it literally just says 17. can we make the numbers look more official?

Agreed — a bare integer undermines the whole document. The usual fix is a short alphanumeric prefix plus a zero-filled counter, so numbers read as part of a system rather than a spreadsheet row. It also gives clients an unambiguous string to quote in emails and bank remittance descriptions, which quietly reduces misapplied payments. Any preference for the prefix? It's usually an abbreviation of the product or company name, and shorter is better.
something short from the brand, you choose. dont make it long

Done — every invoice number now carries the LGR- prefix followed by the zero-filled counter, so number 217 renders as LGR-000217 on documents, emails, and the client portal. I picked LGR- because it's three characters, reads unmistakably as ours, and abbreviates the brand without spelling it out. The prefix is display-level formatting: internally we still store the bare integer, which keeps sorting, gap detection, and reporting trivial, and means presentation can evolve later without rewriting history. I also updated the overdue notice template so the number sits in the header at full size instead of buried in the fine print — that alone makes the document look considerably more official. Search accepts either form, with or without the prefix, so nobody has to remember the rule, and old emails quoting bare numbers still resolve.
perfect, that looks way more legit already

It's live. The next invoice you create picks up the format automatically, and existing invoices re-render with it too since it's presentation-only. If any client references an old bare number, search still resolves it, so there's no transition pain. I'll keep an eye on the nightly accrual job's first few runs as well.
third client this month asked if they can just pay with a card instead of a bank transfer. we did the demos — time to actually pick something and build it

Agreed, we have everything we need to decide. To recap the field: the big general-purpose processors bring brand recognition and superb documentation, but their invoicing primitives are an afterthought — we'd be rebuilding partial payments, balance math, and reconciliation on top of raw charges, and owning that translation layer forever. The invoicing-first gateways flip the trade: hosted payment pages that attach directly to an invoice reference, partial payments as a first-class concept, and webhook events that already speak in invoice terms. Fees are within a rounding error of each other at your volume, so the decision should be weighted almost entirely toward integration fit and reconciliation quality rather than headline pricing. From the demos, the smaller invoice-first option was the one where the payment page already understood our world without any mapping code on our side. You seemed to lean that way in the room — still feel that way?
yeah the smaller one from the demo. setup looked way lighter and the payment page already showed the invoice lines

Agreed — we're going with Brindlepay as the card payment gateway. What sold it for me beyond the demo: their hosted payment page accepts our invoice reference directly, so a client clicking Pay lands on a page that already shows the line items and the exact balance including any accrued charges, and their webhook events arrive pre-tied to that reference, which makes reconciliation a lookup instead of a matching heuristic. Partial payments are first-class too, which matters since your clients sometimes pay deposits. Onboarding on their side is a business verification form plus a settlement bank account — typically a couple of business days to clear. On our side the integration is a payment button on the invoice page and a webhook receiver that transitions invoices to paid; I'll build it behind a feature flag so we can enable it client by client instead of flipping everyone at once.
how long till a client can actually pay?
ok. different topic but related — we're migrating off the old spreadsheet system and clients already have invoice numbers from it, up in the four thousands. the new system cant reuse those

Good catch, and worth settling before any card payment references a number. If the new sequence ever overlaps the legacy range we'd eventually mint a number a client has already seen on an old document, and their bookkeeper will file the new invoice against the old one — that's a genuinely painful support conversation, and worse if a payment has already been matched to it. The clean fix is to start our counter safely above everything the spreadsheet era ever issued, with margin for surprises. What's the highest number the old system reached, as best you can tell?
highest I can find is 4977 but honestly there might be a few strays above that, the sheet was a mess

Then we won't start at the next integer — we'll start the counter at 5081, which clears the highest number you found with a hundred-plus of headroom for any strays hiding in old tabs or emailed copies. The gap costs nothing: invoice numbers need to be unique and increasing, not dense, and no client will ever notice the jump. I've seeded the sequence so the first invoice issued from the new system takes 5081 and everything increments from there, and I've added a validation that refuses manual entry of anything at or below the legacy ceiling, so an imported stray can never collide with a newly issued document. If a genuinely higher legacy number ever surfaces from some forgotten tab, we bump the floor before it becomes a duplicate rather than after — cheap insurance against the sheet's messiness.
perfect. flag me when the sandbox works

Will do. Sandbox target is end of week; I'll send you a test payment link against a dummy invoice so you can walk the exact flow a client sees. The verification paperwork goes in today so the waiting period runs in parallel with the build.
I keep logging into the app just to see if anything got paid. can it just email me a summary instead?

Yes, and this is a classic quality-of-life win: a scheduled digest of payment activity so the app comes to you instead of the other way around. The content I'd start with: payments received since the last digest with client and amount, invoices that newly went overdue, anything sent, and a headline pair of numbers — total collected and total outstanding — so the first two lines tell you whether it was a good week. Frequency is the real design decision. Daily digests rot into noise and get filtered within a month; monthly is so stale it becomes a report rather than a pulse. Weekly is the sweet spot for a business your size — frequent enough to act on, rare enough to stay welcome in the inbox. The other choice is which day it lands, which matters more than people expect for whether it gets read or skimmed into the void.
weekly is right. what day though

Monday morning, seven o'clock in your account timezone. It frames the week ahead — you see who paid and who's newly overdue right when you're planning what to chase — whereas end-of-week digests arrive when you've mentally clocked out and get archived unread. Fresh-week framing is the whole trick with these emails.
can it include the biggest outstanding invoices too, the ones I should actually chase

Good instinct — that turns the digest from a rearview mirror into a to-do list. I'll add a chase list section: the largest outstanding balances ranked by amount, each with client name, how many days past due, and a one-click link into the invoice. I'd cap it at five entries so the email stays scannable; a wall of debtors is demoralizing and stops being actionable. Alongside it, simple aging buckets — current, one to thirty days, thirty-plus — give you the shape of the problem at a glance without any spreadsheet work. Everything links back into the app so the email never tries to be the interface, just the trigger. If a week has nothing outstanding, the section collapses to a single congratulatory line, which honestly is the outcome worth designing for.
dont make it long though, I'll stop reading it

One screen, hard limit. Headline numbers, payments in, newly overdue, chase list, done — no banners, no tips, no product news. Plain-text friendly so it renders anywhere. It ships with next week's release, and the first one will land the following Monday.
the reminder emails are too agressive. first one goes out the morning after the due date and it already threatens the late fee. clients are annoyed

That sequencing is doing us real damage — the first touch after a due date sets the tone for the whole relationship, and opening with a penalty threat reads as debt collection rather than a nudge. Most invoices that end up paid late are paid within the first week or two, usually for innocent reasons: the contact was on holiday, an approval needed a second signature, the email landed in a folder nobody checks. Threatening that window doesn't speed anyone up; it just burns goodwill we'll want later. I'd restructure the whole thing as an escalation ladder: a friendly note at the due date, a firmer reminder a few days in, and only then any mention of consequences. Separately, we should decide how long the fee itself holds off — right now it technically starts accruing immediately, which contradicts any softer copy we write on top of it. How generous do you want that window to be?
a week feels too short but two weeks is a pushover. split the difference?

Split it is — we'll use a 9-day grace window: the late fee only begins accruing on day 10 after the due date, and every email before that point can honestly say no fee has been applied yet. Nine days covers the innocent delays — a holiday week plus a slow approval — without stretching into territory where waiting becomes a strategy. The reminder ladder now lines up with it cleanly: a friendly note on the due date, a firmer one on day 5, and a final heads-up on day 8 that says plainly the fee starts in two days, which gives every client one clean last exit before anything accrues. And because the window is now policy rather than accident, I've surfaced it on the client-facing invoice page too, so the rules are visible before anyone is late instead of revealed afterward like a trap.
good. also the subject lines are SO shouty. 'INVOICE OVERDUE - ACTION REQUIRED' like relax

Softened all three. The due-date note becomes 'A quick reminder about invoice …', day 5 becomes 'Following up on invoice …', and day 8 becomes 'Heads-up: a late fee starts soon on invoice …'. Sentence case throughout, no exclamation marks, client's first name in the greeting. The firmness lives in the body copy where there's room for nuance, not screamed from the subject line.
btw while your in there — recurring invoices. when do those actualy get created? one showed up and went out the same second, I had no chance to even look at it

Right now they're generated and dispatched at the same moment the schedule fires — that's the bare-bones stopgap from months back, and what you hit is exactly its known flaw: there is no review window at all. The better pattern is to generate each occurrence as a draft ahead of its billing date, so it sits where you can see it, fix a line item or a stale address, and only then goes out. The open question is lead time: too short and you'll miss the window on a busy week, too long and the amounts drift stale if the scope changed mid-month. Where's the balance for you?
enough time to actually catch mistakes but not so early the amounts go stale. you call it

Calling it: each recurring invoice is generated as a draft 6 days before its billing date. That's close to a working week — long enough that a draft created on a Friday still gets eyes on it before it matters, short enough that scope changes from earlier in the month have already settled by the time the draft exists. The draft appears in your invoices list with a scheduled badge showing its billing date, and it sits there until you review and send it, so nothing leaves without your eyes on it — the opposite failure of the one you just hit. If that review step turns into busywork as the retainer count grows, we can add selective automation later, but the default should start safe. I also added a dashboard line listing drafts awaiting their billing date, so nothing is ever created invisibly again.
love it. ship the email changes today if you can

Shipped. The new subject lines and ladder timing apply to anything going overdue from today onward; already-overdue invoices finish out the old sequence to avoid double-sending anyone. The recurring draft behavior takes effect from each schedule's next occurrence. I'll watch the send logs this week to confirm the day-5 and day-8 steps fire on time.
clients are paying by card now which is great but I genuinely can't tell when the money reaches our actual bank. invoice says paid and then... mystery

That mystery is the settlement layer. When a client pays, the gateway holds the funds briefly and then transfers them to your bank in batches — the invoice flips to paid immediately, but the cash arrives on the batch schedule, which is why the two never line up in your head. The good news is the schedule is ours to configure. Rolling daily payouts move money fastest but produce a dribble of small deposits that make bank reconciliation tedious: every day's statement line has to be matched against whichever payments happened to clear that day. A fixed weekly payout batches everything into one predictable deposit — slightly slower on average, but your bank statement gets one clean line a week and bookkeeping becomes a five-minute job instead of a scavenger hunt. At your payment volume the cash-flow difference between the two is negligible, so this is genuinely a bookkeeping-preference question. Which way do you lean?
fixed day, no contest. matching a million tiny deposits sounds like hell

Set — payouts now land every Thursday as a single weekly settlement batch covering everything cleared since the previous one. Thursday specifically because card payments cluster early in the week when your clients run their own bill payments, so a Thursday cutoff captures the bulk of the week's activity while still landing before the weekend — and if a transfer ever slips a day it arrives Friday rather than sitting somewhere over the weekend. Each batch comes with a settlement report listing exactly which invoice payments it contains, and I'm pulling that report into the app, so a deposit on your bank statement can be expanded into its component invoices without leaving Ledgerly. Fees are netted out per batch rather than per transaction, which keeps the statement line matching the report total to the cent — no more mystery between paid and arrived.
can the dashboard show the next expected payout? amount and date
one more thing. the recurring drafts are great BUT now I'm clicking approve on a pile of identical little retainer invoices every month. feels dumb

That's the review window doing its job too indiscriminately — a fixed retainer that's identical every month doesn't need your eyes, while a large or unusual invoice absolutely does. The standard fix is an amount threshold: below it, drafts go out automatically on their billing date with no action from you; at or above it, they wait for explicit approval. You keep the safety net exactly where mistakes are expensive and lose the busywork where they aren't. Where should the line sit — what do the routine retainers actually run?
the routine ones are all under two grand ish. the scary ones are way above that

Then the threshold goes at $2,400 — recurring drafts below $2,400 auto-send on their billing date, and anything at or above it stays in drafts until you approve it. I set it a comfortable step above your routine retainer range so a modest price bump on an existing retainer doesn't suddenly start demanding approvals, while everything you called scary is still held. Auto-sent invoices are flagged as such in the activity log, so you can always audit what went out on its own, and the monthly summary counts them separately from the ones you approved by hand. If a normally-small schedule ever produces an unusually large draft — a one-off expense line, say — it crosses the line and waits for you, which is precisely the case the safety net exists for. The threshold lives in settings as a single field if your retainer sizes ever drift upward.
that's exactly it. thanks

Done and live. The payout card ships with tonight's deploy, the auto-send rule takes effect for the next round of recurring drafts, and I'll drop you a note after the first batch settles so you can see the settlement report expansion working against a real deposit.
my accountant wants the client list as a csv. with billing emails and totals per client

Easy enough. I'd give the export these columns: client name, billing email, billing address, total invoiced, total collected, and current outstanding balance — that's everything an accountant typically reconciles against. One question before I build it: should the totals be lifetime, or scoped to a date range like the current financial year? Accountants usually want the year, but lifetime is the simpler mental model.
lifetime totals is fine she can filter herself. oh and she complained the dates in the app look american, drives her nuts

Both fixable, and they're actually two different problems wearing the same coat. In the app UI, dates should follow the viewer's locale — day-first for most of the world, month-first where that's the convention — which we get nearly free from the browser's locale APIs, so her complaint about the on-screen format disappears without a setting. The CSV is a different animal: a file gets opened by whatever spreadsheet tool with whatever regional configuration, and locale-formatted dates are exactly how a column silently flips day and month and corrupts a reconciliation. The boring, correct answer there is ISO format — year, month, day — which every spreadsheet parses unambiguously and sorts correctly as plain text. So: locale-aware display in the app, strict ISO in every export, and the two never trade places. It's the difference between a date meant for human eyes and one meant for machines.
iso in the file is fine, she can format it however she wants once its in her spreadsheet
works. she also wants it monthly without asking me, can it just email her

Done — a scheduled export that emails the CSV to a named recipient on the first of each month, using the same generator so the columns never drift from the manual version. She's set as the recipient, you're cc'd so you always know it went out, and if generation ever fails you get an alert instead of silence.

queries.question: What's our complete late fee setup now — how much do we charge on overdue invoices, and when does it actually start applying?
queries.gold_answer: Overdue invoices accrue a late fee of 2.75% per month on the outstanding balance, but there is a 9-day grace window: the fee only starts accruing on day 10 after the due date.
queries.judge_notes: Rubric: correct iff the answer states BOTH the 2.75% per month rate AND the 9-day grace window (fee starting around day 10 past due). Partial iff it states exactly one of the two facts correctly and does not misstate the other. Incorrect iff it gives a different rate or window, claims a flat fee, or claims the fee starts immediately at the due date.

queries.question: Which provider did we end up going with for card payments, and on what schedule does the money actually reach our bank account?
queries.gold_answer: Card payments go through Brindlepay, and payouts settle as one weekly batch that lands every Thursday.
queries.judge_notes: Rubric: correct iff the answer names Brindlepay as the gateway AND says payouts arrive weekly on Thursday. Partial iff it gives exactly one of the two correctly and does not misstate the other. Incorrect iff it names Stripe or any other provider as chosen, or gives a different payout day/cadence (e.g. daily payouts or Monday).

queries.question: Remind me how our invoice numbering works — the format clients see and where the sequence started counting?
queries.gold_answer: Invoice numbers are displayed with the LGR- prefix and a zero-filled counter, and the sequence was started at 5081 to stay clear of the legacy spreadsheet numbers.
queries.judge_notes: Rubric: correct iff the answer gives BOTH the LGR- prefix format AND that the counter/sequence starts at 5081. Partial iff exactly one of the two is given correctly and the other is not misstated. Incorrect iff it gives a different prefix or a different starting number (e.g. 4977, 4978, or 1).

queries.question: How does recurring invoicing behave end to end now — when do the invoices get created, and which ones go out without me approving them?
queries.gold_answer: Each recurring invoice is generated as a draft 6 days before its billing date. Drafts below $2,400 auto-send on the billing date, while anything at or above $2,400 stays in drafts until manually approved.
queries.judge_notes: Rubric: correct iff the answer states BOTH that recurring invoices are generated as drafts 6 days before the billing date AND the $2,400 auto-send threshold (below auto-sends, at/above waits for approval). Partial iff exactly one of the two facts is correct and the other is not misstated. Incorrect iff it gives a different lead time or threshold, says everything auto-sends, or says everything requires approval, or describes only the old same-moment stopgap behavior as current.
